model_findings.description: The new proxy is documented as localhost-only, but the CLI exposes `--host` directly to `uvicorn.run` without rejecting non-loopback values or requiring an explicit remote-access opt-in. Since the service has no authentication and can launch local agent CLIs from incoming requests, `ab serve --openai --host 0.0.0.0` can expose a local subprocess execution bridge to the network by accident.

The chat request model accepts an unbounded `messages` list with unbounded string content, and each accepted request starts a backend subprocess that may run for up to 120 seconds. A client can submit very large prompts or many-message payloads to consume memory, CPU, process slots, and backend quota; the proxy should enforce request and prompt size limits before flattening and dispatching to local CLIs.

The Gemini, Claude, and Hermes backends pass the full user prompt as command-line arguments (`--prompt=...`, positional prompt, or `--oneshot=...`). This exposes potentially sensitive request content through local process listings and also makes large requests fail at OS argument-length limits; these backends should pass prompt content through stdin or a temporary file with controlled permissions.

The request model allows arbitrary extra OpenAI fields but the endpoint ignores `stream=true` and still returns a normal non-streaming completion with HTTP 200. OpenAI-compatible clients that request streaming will receive an unexpected response shape; the proxy should either implement streaming or reject `stream=true` with a clear unsupported-parameter error.

The new gate only requires `sentence` and `error`, while treating both `answer` and `correction` as optional. That means an `error-correction` item with `sentence:` and `error:` but no corrected form passes `activity_schema`, even though the existing activity schema test rejects this shape and the parser/renderer will emit `correctForm=""`. This misses a real writer mistake rather than only enforcing the alias contract.
